markdown:
## Techniques Shape Visual Narratives


<definition term="Visual techniques ">Visual techniques are the **deliberate design choices** that shape how we **experience** a visual text.</definition>

1. Just like **diction** and **structure** shape a written text, there are techniques that** **shape a visual one.
2. Choices of visual techniques include: 
  1. **Layout**: How elements are arranged on the page or screen.
  2. **Typography**: The style and size of text.
  3. **Color**: The use of color to create mood or highlight meaning.
  4. **Panel Arrangement**: In graphic novels, how panels are organized to control pacing.
  5. **Stage Directions**: In plays, instructions for actors' movements and positioning.

<callout type="note">Visual techniques are not just decorative they **guide your interpretation** of the text.</callout>

## Why Visual Techniques Matter


1. Visual techniques do **three** key things: 
  1. **Direct Attention**: A character in bright red against a grey background instantly stands out.
  2. **Control Pacing**: Fast scene cuts or tightly packed panels create urgency. Wide empty spaces slow things down.
  3. **Mirror Meaning**: A cracked mirror in a scene isn’t just decoration it might reflect a fractured identity or relationship.

![Red and purple stands out from a grey background](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/2adb23d0d98a98cdb9cb0e5af3ba2082c99deee8-1328x970.png)

<callout type="tip">* When you analyse visuals, you’re asking the **same core question**: 
* **Why did the creator make this choice** and **what does it do**?</callout>

### Layout


<definition term="Layout">The spatial arrangement of elements on a page or screen. It **guides the reader's eye** and controls the flow of information.</definition>

#### Vocabulary to Describe Layout


| Centered | Marginalized |
| --- | --- |
| Foreground | Background |
| Symmetrical | Asymmetrical |
| Balanced | Unbalanced  |
| Framed | Isolated  |
| Cluttered | Minimal |

<callout type="example">![Example magazine layout](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/caa0ff6948ff8b15787c00f4815f7ee94125e282-2738x1672.png)

* In a magazine ad, the product could be placed at the **center**. 
* This **draws immediate attention**, while supporting text is placed around it.</callout>

### Typography


<definition term="Typography">The style, size, and arrangement of text. Typography can **convey tone** and **emphasize key ideas**.</definition>

#### Vocabulary to Describe Typography


| Serif | Sans-Serif |
| --- | --- |
| Bold | Italic |
| Block capitals | Script font  |
| Decorative | Clean |
| Large-scale | Small-scale |

<callout type="example">![I.T movie poster](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9a1536250a5a7ff70836daf19722646f7b297586-906x1356.png)

In a horror movie poster, jagged, red letters create a sense of danger and urgency.</callout>

### Color


<definition term="Color">The use of color to** influence emotion** or **signal meaning**</definition>

![Color psychology](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/4782e238916d8fd3b7819d1d38a6fa55e4797834-2370x1264.png)

<callout type="example">High contrast / Muted tones / Saturated / Monochrome / Symbolic color use</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">"The use of **high-contrast** red and black mirrors the tension in the scene."</callout>

### Panel Arrangement


<definition term="Panel arrangement">The organization of panels in graphic novels or comics. Panel arrangement **controls pacing** and **emphasizes key moments**.</definition>

#### Vocabulary to Describe Panel Arrangement


| Wide panel | Close panel |
| --- | --- |
| Tight sequence | Fragmented sequence  |
| Left-to-right flow | Non-linear sequence  |

<callout type="example">![Examples of different panel arrangements](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a9468583187325d096eab43b4d3659b5ddd044f5-700x803.png)

A single, large panel might be used to highlight a dramatic event, while a series of small panels can create a sense of rapid action.</callout>

### Stage Directions (Drama)


<definition term="Stage directions">Instructions in a play's script about actors' movements and positioning.</definition>

#### Vocabulary to Describe Stage Directions


| Crosses to | Retreats from |
| --- | --- |
| [Pause] | [Aside]  |
| Proxemics (use of space)  | Blocking (movement pattern)  |
| Levels (standing/sitting) | Levels (height difference)  |

<callout type="tip">Stage directions **reveal character relationships** and **set the tone** of a scene.</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">![Factory text](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/024689caa88ffd1f7b0ada09f3e72512f0b3a5c3-1228x1592.png)

* The layout places the factory **front** and **center**, dominating the viewer’s field of vision and immediately **framing** it as the **source of the problem**. 
* The **monochromatic color scheme** of greys and blacks creates a **bleak**, **lifeless** mood, while the bold, all-caps **sans-serif **headline “Breathe This In” feels **harsh** and **confrontational**. 
* Together, these visual techniques create a **sense of urgency** and **blame**, positioning the factory as a **direct threat** to the viewer’s health.</callout>

<callout type="tip">Instead of saying "the colors are bright," specify "the use of vibrant reds and **yellows** creates a sense of urgency."</callout>
